title: Add option to not switch cgroup when user doesn't own it
body:
Hello,
I've added a command line option which makes it possible to not switch cgroup when user doesn't own it.

We need this change in our environment because we're using podman for PBS jobs and if podman changes cgroup to user.slice of user who started the job rather than keep the PBS' cgroup, we can't properly monitor how much resources the job is using.

I hope this change looks alright to you and can be merged

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *zv0n* To complete the [pull request process](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process), please assign **baude** You can assign the PR to them by writing `/assign @baude` in a comment when ready. The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
/assign @baude
@giuseppe PTAL
Not sure what PBS is?
@giuseppe is on PTO right now, but I thought we had a mechanism to tell the container to not use cgroups, which seems like what you want here?
@rhatdan Yes, `--cgroups=disabled` (though this only supports crun right now)
@zv0n as @mheon pointed out, we already have `--cgroups=disabled`.  Does that work for your use case or is there anything missing?
I think we've tried that with 1.9 and it didn't quite do the trick. I don't have access to our environment right now, but I'll test it out tomorrow and let you know what does/doesn't work
If it does not work, lets work on it to see if we can fix whatever is broken to make it work.

Hi, I've tested `--cgroups disabled` and it doesn't work. I suspect it's because the portion of SetupRootles, which changes CGroup, only checks whether CGroupManager is systemd and even if CGroupManager ISN'T systemd, if the current system has systemd as init, the function changes CGroup.

Side note - I was also informed that some of our users want shared pid namespace (`--pid host`) for easy communication between containers, which isn't possible with `--cgroups disabled`. I realize this is a very very edge case usage, so I understand if you don't want an additional command line parameter for that, but if it'd be possible to add this option (have cgroups enabled, but skip cgroup switch to user.slice) to config file for example, we'd really appreciate it :)

> Hi, I've tested `--cgroups disabled` and it doesn't work. I suspect it's because the portion of SetupRootles, which changes CGroup, only checks whether CGroupManager is systemd and even if CGroupManager ISN'T systemd, if the current system has systemd as init, the function changes CGroup.

I think we should skip the check when `--cgroups=disabled`.




> Side note - I was also informed that some of our users want shared pid namespace (`--pid host`) for easy communication between containers, which isn't possible with `--cgroups disabled`. I realize this is a very very edge case usage, so I understand if you don't want an additional command line parameter for that, but if it'd be possible to add this option (have cgroups enabled, but skip cgroup switch to user.slice) to config file for example, we'd really appreciate it :)

How would we be able to terminate the container without cgroups and pid namespace?  The idea with `--pid=host` is that we can get the list of processes from the cgroup.  If we have no cgroup and no pid namespace, it won't be possible to terminate a container, as processes can escape.

I see, well I guess we'll just tell our users that they can't have everything they want :D

> I think we should skip the check when `--cgroups=disabled`.

Should I create an issue then? I think changes in this PR are irrelevant at this point and I don't know from which variable to get cgroups setting
@zv0n Can you modify your patch to just not do the check if --cgroups=disabled  We should get that partial fix in for now.

@rhatdan I've tried going through the code and the only way I see that this could be done is if the cgroups option was moved to rootflags. Right now it is in command flags and I don't see a way to access those from root.go. I've tried moving the cgroups option to rootFlags, but don't know from where to get correct default value, could you please point me in the right direction? Also would moving cgroups to rootFlags be acceptable to you?
@giuseppe WDYT?

Moving `--cgroups` to a root flag does not seem feasible to me.

Is there any way to rework the logic in `setupRootless()` to not require a flag?
Maybe `--cgroup-manager`?
@giuseppe so something like `--cgroup-manager disabled`, which would also automatically set `--cgroups disabled`?

> @giuseppe so something like `--cgroup-manager disabled`, which would also automatically set `--cgroups disabled`?

yes, I think that could work.  @mheon what do you think?

After trying to implement this, I think it's not possible to change `--cgroups` based on `--cgroups-manager` because when `--cgroups` is being parsed from command line the function creates a new separate configuration object from default values. (`cmd/podman/common/create.go:14` calls `PodmanConfig()` which in turn calls `newPodmanConfig()`)
So, anyone here have any idea how to achieve this? Because so far, short of completely rewriting the CLI parsing system, I don't see a way :D
@mheon @baude PTAL

@zv0n: PR needs rebase.

<details>

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

I don't actually see what you're saying with being unable to use `--cgroup-manager` to set `--cgroups`. I don't follow how we're creating a new config object - it's called in a `sync.Once` to ensure we only ever ensure one config?

@mheon I might be wrong, I haven't programmed in Go much, but to me it seems that when `flags.AddFlagSet(common.GetCreateFlags(&cliVals)` is called in `cmd/podman/containers/run.go` a new config `containerConfig` is created in `cmd/podman/common/crate.go`.

This new config is then used on lines 58-62 in `create.go` to provide default value for `cf.CGroupsMode`.

This is a problem as I'd like to edit `containerConfig` based on value set to `cfg.Engine.CgroupManager` in `cmd/podman/root.go`, so `containerConfig.Cgroups()` would return `disabled` in appropriate situation. But since the `containerConfig` var is created in `create.go` I can't change anything in it from `root.go` (at least I think, again, I'm not a Go Pro (heh :D)).

I also can't change the value based on `cfg.Engine.CgroupManager` without extensive rewrites. As the code exists now, it only expects `cgroupfs` or `systemd`. So my idea for this PR was:

If `--cgroupsManager` is set to `disabled`, change `containerConfig` so `containerConfig.Cgroups()` would return `disabled`, then change `cfg.Engine.CgroupManager` to its default value `cgroupfs` so as to not break existing code.

But as I have said, I'm running into issues with changing `containerConfig`.

If I'm doing something wrong/stupidly, please tell me, I'd like to learn, thanks!
Hi, could someone please tell me if what I think is right?

I strongly suspect you're looking in the wrong place - there is no Engine field in `entities.PodmanConfig`, which is what we care about. I think you're looking at the containers/common config, which is loaded in from configuration file. That is then overridden by the `entities.PodmanConfig` settings in `cmd/podman/registry` that are sourced from CLI flags.

Nevermind. Apparently `PodmanConfig` embeds a containers/common config, which doesn't seem strictly correct to me, but oh well...

It looks like we can't get in to change the value of that config before Libpod is initialized, and we can't alter Libpod's configuration once it's up.

We may need to add a third cgroup-manager option, `disabled`, to effectively force everything to `--cgroups=disabled`. @giuseppe Thoughts?
So, should I create an issue for this? This looks like it would be a substantial change to the code base and I don't feel confident enough in my Go/Cgroup knowledge to do this myself :D

Sure.

On Mon, Oct 19, 2020, 08:28 zv0n <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> So, should I create an issue for this? This looks like it would be a
> substantial change to the code base and I don't feel confident enough in my
> Go/Cgroup knowledge to do this myself :D
>
